images.caption:
Figure 1:Overview of PLT loop-count selection. Left: standard sequential looping
increases latency and KV-cache memory with the loop count, whereas PLT uses a
cross-loop position offset and shared-KV G-SWA to keep both costs nearly
constant. Middle: each added loop trades marginal refinement gain against the
CLP-induced offset mismatch. The gain peaks early and then shrinks while the
offset cost remains roughly stable, makingr=2r=2the preferred operating point.
Right: per-loop diagnostics explain this choice. Loop 2 shows coherent
hidden-state movement, changed attention routing, increased representation
diversity, and broad token-level refinement, while loop 3 is more redundant and
less productive.

Figure 2:Step sizeŒ¥(r)\delta^{(r)}(top-left), angular changecos‚Å°Œ∏(r)\cos\theta^{(r)}(top-right), scale-free effective rankerank‚Äã(ùê°(r))\mathrm{erank}(\mathbf{h}^{(r)})(bottom-left), and fixed-point gapŒîFP(r)\Delta_{\text{FP}}^{(r)}(bottom-right)
as a function of loop indexrr. Lines: PLT2/PLT3/PLT4(trainedR=2,3,4R{=}2,3,4); the baseline (R=1R{=}1) is shown
where defined. Shaded bands are 95% CIs over 500 samples (often narrower than
the markers); the dotted line in (c) marks the embedding. Effective rank peaks
at loop 2 and declines for every deeper loop; successive updates are oscillatory
(cos‚Å°Œ∏(r)<0\cos\theta^{(r)}<0) through the refinement loops, and the step size shrinks to
a mid-depth minimum before rebounding at the final (output) loop.

Figure 3:The gain‚Äìcost scissors (PLT4). The per-loop refinement gainŒî‚Äãp(r)\Delta p^{(r)}(output-distribution KL; left axis, log) collapses after loop 2
and never recovers, whereas the intrinsic CLP offset costŒ©(r)\Omega^{(r)}(Equation¬Ý6; right axis) stays high and roughly fixed. At every
extra loop the offset cost exceeds the per-loop gain by3030‚Äì45√ó45\times, so the
fixed offset tax dominates the shrinking refinement beyond loop 2. 500 samples;
shaded bands are 95% CIs.

Figure 4:Head√ó\timeshead cosine similarity of the per-head attention
distributions at loopsr=1,2,3r=1,2,3(PLT3, 500 held-out samples; self-similarity
on the diagonal masked). Brighter cells indicate more redundant heads;sim¬Ø\overline{\mathrm{sim}}is the mean off-diagonal similarity. Heads grow
progressively more redundant across loops.

Figure 5:Mean attention entropy‚Ñã(r)\mathcal{H}^{(r)}(left),
inter-loop KL divergenceDKL(r)D_{\text{KL}}^{(r)}(middle),
and mean G-SWA gateg¬Ø(r)\bar{g}^{(r)}(right; the weight on the global loop-1
branch) as a function of loop indexrr(PLT2/PLT3/PLT4). The inter-loop
KL drops sharply after loop 2 and stays low, indicating that attention routing
largely freezes once the second loop completes; the gate stays well above0.50.5at every loop.

Figure 6:Logit-lens ground-truth token rank (left), inter-loop output
KL divergenceŒî‚Äãp(r)\Delta p^{(r)}(middle, log scale), and output entropy‚Ñã‚Äã(p(r))\mathcal{H}(p^{(r)})(right) as a function of loop indexrr(PLT2/PLT3/PLT4).
Predictions sharpen monotonically with depth, but theper-loopchangeŒî‚Äãp(r)\Delta p^{(r)}collapses after loop 2 (the small uptick at the final loop is
output readout rather than new refinement), marking the onset of diminishing
returns.

Figure 7:How post-context refinement is distributed across the extra loops of
PLT4. Each bar splits the refinement carried by the refinement loops
(r‚â•2r\geq 2) into its loop-2, loop-3, and loop-4 shares, under three independent
lenses: the output shiftŒî‚Äãp(r)\Delta p^{(r)}, the inter-loop attention re-routingDKL(r)D_{\mathrm{KL}}^{(r)}, and the per-token peak-contribution loopr‚àó=arg‚Å°maxr‚Å°Œî‚Äãp(r)r^{*}=\arg\max_{r}\Delta p^{(r)}. The middle extra loop (loop 3, grey) carries
thesmallestshare on every lens: genuine refinement concentrates at
loop 2,where attention re-routing and effective rank also peak,while loop 4‚Äôs
large output-side share is final readout rather than representational
enrichment (its effective rank is the lowest;Figure¬Ý2c).
(Loop 1 builds context and is excluded to isolate refinement.)
